implausible_knowledge: “The king was riding through the land, seeing how his people lived and keeping an eye on the borders, for there is always a neighbor somewhere to test a king.”
error: The sister, without leaving her doorstep, said, “Hush—King Matthias is riding through the land now, seeing how his people live and keeping an eye on the borders; he’ll hear us.”
erroneous_event: People began to say the judge was waiting for a share. <error>The sister, without leaving her doorstep, said, “Hush—King Matthias is riding through the land now, seeing how his people live and keeping an eye on the borders; he’ll hear us.”</error> The rich brother did not want to share with anyone, not even a taste, and the quarrel grew so noisy that it reached the ears of King Matthias.
story:
In the time of King Matthias, in one county village, there lived a brother and a sister. The brother was rich. He drove great herds of bulls to Vienna for the Emperor, who loved delicious perkelt. The sister lived nearby and was poor. She lived without a husband, not because she was foolish, but because she was clever, and some men were afraid of a clever woman. The neighbors whispered, “What a bad brother. He has gold, and his sister has nothing.”

At last the brother “helped” her by giving her the worst cow in his herd, a creature he was sure would die. But the sister was smart and patient. She fed the cow, cared for it, and soon it grew strong and calved—a beautiful little bull. When he heard, the brother hurried over and claimed the calf for himself. “I gave you a cow,” he said, “not a bull.” The sister refused. Again the neighbors said, “Her brother is a bad man.”

They went to the judge. The judge liked delicious perkelt very much, and he thought a long time. He thought so long that a whole year passed, and the calf grew into a handsome, snorting bull. People began to say the judge was waiting for a share. The sister, without leaving her doorstep, said, “Hush—King Matthias is riding through the land now, seeing how his people live and keeping an eye on the borders; he’ll hear us.” The rich brother did not want to share with anyone, not even a taste, and the quarrel grew so noisy that it reached the ears of King Matthias.

The king was riding through the land, seeing how his people lived and keeping an eye on the borders, for there is always a neighbor somewhere to test a king. He too loved a well-cooked perkelt. When he came to the village and saw the great bull, he asked at once, “Whose bull is that? I will buy it.” The judge turned pale. He pointed to the brother and the sister, who fell to arguing again. King Matthias raised his hand for silence. “No more shouting,” he said. “I will ask three questions. Whoever answers best will have the bull.

“What is the fastest thing in the world? Who is the richest? What is most desired?”

The rich brother spoke first: “My stallion is the fastest in the world. The richest is King Matthias himself. The most desired is money—the Emperor’s golden florins.”

Then the sister answered: “Fastest is a human thought. In a moment I think of Buda, then Vyšehrad, and in an instant I fly from Buda to Vyšehrad. Richest is the earth. It gives water and food and never grows poor. Most desired is the sun. It lights the world and warms the earth and all people.”

King Matthias loved cleverness, for he was clever himself. He smiled and gave the bull to the sister. In good humor he also sat down to a bowl of perkelt. Then he appointed the smart girl to collect taxes in that province, because an honest mind is good for a king. She worked quickly and fairly and discovered that her rich brother had not been paying his taxes. He had to pay what he owed, and so, piece by piece, his unfair wealth fell away. He learned humility; she earned respect.

So remember: never offend a smart woman.
